The p.R521C and other NLS-domain mutations cause cytoplasmic FUS mislocalization. Any ALS presentation in a patient under 25 should prompt urgent FUS genetic testing. Survival is generally shorter than typical ALS. FUS-ALS is autosomal dominant in most cases.

questions.question: A 42-year-old man has progressive bilateral leg spasticity and gait difficulty for 8 years. He cannot run. DTRs are brisk with bilateral Babinski signs. Lower extremity MRI shows no compressive lesion. No weakness in the arms. Brain MRI shows a thin corpus callosum. His brother is similarly affected. Which gene is most likely?
choices: SPAST (spastin — SPG4, most common HSP)
ATL1 (atlastin — SPG3A, early childhood onset)
CYP7B1 (SPG5, complicated HSP with cerebellar ataxia)
REEP1 (SPG31)
questions.explanation: Hereditary spastic paraplegia type 4 (SPG4), caused by SPAST mutations, is the most common HSP subtype, accounting for ~40% of all autosomal dominant HSP. It causes 'pure' HSP (bilateral leg spasticity with bladder urgency; arms usually spared) but can have a thin corpus callosum as an incidental finding. Onset is variable (childhood to late adult). SPG3A (ATL1) typically presents in early childhood and is the second most common AD-HSP. CYP7B1/SPG5 causes cerebellar ataxia in addition to spasticity ('complicated' HSP).

questions.question: A 20-year-old woman presents after her first episode of foot drop following a 2-hour bus ride with her leg crossed. Nerve conduction studies show focal conduction block at the fibular head with mild background slowing of motor conduction velocities bilaterally. She had a similar episode 2 years ago. Her father has had multiple pressure palsies. What is the diagnosis?
choices: Charcot-Marie-Tooth type 1A (PMP22 duplication)
Hereditary neuropathy with liability to pressure palsies (HNPP — PMP22 deletion)
Multifocal motor neuropathy
Peroneal nerve entrapment neuropathy (sporadic)
questions.explanation: HNPP is caused by deletion of one PMP22 copy on chromosome 17p12. Clinically: recurrent focal mononeuropathies at common pressure points (peroneal at fibular head, ulnar at elbow, median at carpal tunnel), each episode typically resolving over weeks. Background NCS shows diffuse but mild slowing (not as severe as CMT1A duplication). Family history of similar episodes is common. CMT1A causes uniform slowing without episodic focal palsy. Spontaneous recovery distinguishes HNPP episodes from irreversible mononeuropathies.

resource.label: GeneReviews: Hereditary Neuropathy with Pressure Palsies

questions.question: A previously healthy 25-year-old man develops rapidly ascending weakness over 5 days, starting with bilateral foot tingling. He had diarrhea 3 weeks earlier. Examination shows areflexia, mild distal weakness, preserved cognition. CSF shows elevated protein (120 mg/dL) with normal WBC count (albuminocytologic dissociation). NCS shows absent H-reflexes, prolonged F-waves, and conduction block. What is the next step?
choices: MRI brain and spine with gadolinium to exclude demyelinating disease
IVIG 2 g/kg over 5 days OR plasmapheresis 5 exchanges over 2 weeks
High-dose IV methylprednisolone for 5 days
Lumbar puncture for IgG index and oligoclonal bands
questions.explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome (AIDP) requires prompt treatment with either IVIG (2 g/kg over 5 days) or therapeutic plasma exchange (5 exchanges over 2 weeks) — both are equally effective. Steroids (corticosteroids) are NOT beneficial in AIDP and are specifically contraindicated — multiple trials showed no benefit or harm. Combined IVIG + PE is no better than either alone. Lumbar puncture (already done) confirms albuminocytologic dissociation. MRI is not the immediate priority when GBS is clinically clear.

questions.question: A 45-year-old woman has had symmetric distal sensorimotor neuropathy for 3 years with progressive worsening. NCS shows slowing of motor conduction velocities and prolonged distal latencies in multiple nerves. CSF protein is 95 mg/dL. She initially responded to a prednisone course but relapsed. Which diagnosis requires consideration of long-term maintenance immunotherapy?
choices: Guillain-Barré syndrome (AIDP) — treatment-responsive remission expected
Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP)
POEMS syndrome (monoclonal plasma cell disorder)
CMT1A (PMP22 duplication) — corticosteroid-responsive demyelinating neuropathy
questions.explanation: CIDP is defined by progressive or relapsing-remitting demyelinating neuropathy lasting >8 weeks, with CSF protein elevation and NCS demyelinating features. Treatment response followed by relapse on steroid taper is characteristic. Long-term maintenance is typically required — options include IVIG (every 3–6 weeks), subcutaneous Ig, or steroids. Efgartigimod and rilzabrutinib are emerging CIDP therapies. CMT1A does not respond to immunotherapy. POEMS requires treatment of the underlying plasma cell clone.

questions.question: A 70-year-old man has a 3-year course of slowly progressive distal leg sensory loss and ataxic gait. Serum IgM is 3,800 mg/dL with an IgM kappa monoclonal spike. Anti-myelin-associated glycoprotein (anti-MAG) antibodies are strongly positive. NCS shows prominent prolongation of distal motor latencies out of proportion to conduction velocity slowing. What is the best characterization?
choices: CIDP — treat with IVIG
Anti-MAG neuropathy associated with IgM MGUS — predominantly sensory, responds better to rituximab than IVIG
Multifocal motor neuropathy — treat with IVIG
AL amyloidosis neuropathy — treat with plasma cell clone-directed therapy
questions.explanation: Anti-MAG neuropathy is a distal, predominantly sensory (ataxic) demyelinating neuropathy associated with IgM MGUS and anti-MAG antibodies. The NCS signature is disproportionately prolonged distal latencies (widened action potential due to terminal demyelination). IVIG has limited efficacy; rituximab (anti-CD20) targeting the IgM-secreting B-cell clone is currently the most effective treatment, with ~50% of patients showing improvement. The slow, ataxia-predominant course and IgM MGUS context distinguish it from CIDP.

questions.question: A 55-year-old man has pure motor, asymmetric arm weakness with wasting of intrinsic hand muscles. No sensory symptoms. NCS shows conduction block in motor nerves not at common entrapment sites. He has anti-GM1 IgM antibodies. DTRs are reduced. No Babinski signs. What treatment is indicated?
choices: Plasma exchange (plasmapheresis)
IVIG 2 g/kg every 4–8 weeks (maintenance)
High-dose prednisone 60 mg/day
Cyclophosphamide plus rituximab
questions.explanation: Multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN) presents as pure motor, asymmetric neuropathy with conduction block on NCS and anti-GM1 IgM antibodies (~50%). It mimics motor neuron disease but has NO upper motor neuron signs. IVIG (2 g/kg induction, then maintenance every 4–8 weeks) is the treatment of choice and is typically highly effective. Unlike CIDP, MMN does NOT respond to plasma exchange or corticosteroids — corticosteroids may actually worsen MMN. This drug-class distinction is a classic board point.

questions.question: A 62-year-old man presents with a painful, length-dependent, small-fiber neuropathy, carpal tunnel syndrome, and a new diagnosis of cardiomyopathy with increased wall thickness on echo. His father died of heart failure at 64. Serum and urine protein electrophoresis are negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: AL amyloidosis (immunoglobulin light chain) — screen for plasma cell dyscrasia
Hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis (hATTR — TTR mutation) — genetic testing indicated
POEMS syndrome — look for VEGF elevation
Fabry disease — measure alpha-galactosidase A activity
questions.explanation: Hereditary TTR amyloidosis (hATTR) classically presents as painful small-fiber peripheral neuropathy (often length-dependent with autonomic features), bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with 'granular sparkling' echo pattern — all in the setting of a negative serum/urine SPEP (ruling out AL). Positive family history strongly supports hereditary TTR. TTR gene sequencing confirms; treatments include tafamidis (TTR stabilizer), patisiran and inotersen (RNA-targeted therapies). Val30Met is the most common worldwide TTR mutation.

questions.question: A 12-year-old boy has burning pain and paresthesias in his hands and feet triggered by exercise and fever. He has angiokeratomas on his lower trunk. Ophthalmology notes cornea verticillata. Renal function shows microalbuminuria. His maternal uncle had similar symptoms. What is the diagnosis and first-line treatment?
choices: Refsum disease (PHYH mutation) — dietary restriction of phytanic acid
Fabry disease (GLA mutation, X-linked) — enzyme replacement therapy with agalsidase
Hereditary transthyretin amyloidosis — tafamidis
HSAN type 1 (SPTLC1) — serine supplementation
questions.explanation: Fabry disease (alpha-galactosidase A deficiency, GLA — X-linked) classically presents in boys with acroparesthesias (burning pain, heat/exercise-triggered), angiokeratomas, cornea verticillata, and progressive renal/cardiac disease. Maternal uncle inheritance is consistent with X-linked. Enzyme replacement therapy (agalsidase alfa or beta) is the standard treatment and reduces neuropathic pain and organ progression. Migalastat (pharmacological chaperone) is an oral alternative for patients with amenable mutations.

questions.question: A 10-year-old boy presents with progressive retinitis pigmentosa, ataxia, peripheral neuropathy, anosmia, and ichthyosis. His serum phytanic acid is markedly elevated. What dietary restriction is essential?
choices: Low-fat diet to reduce all dietary lipids
Strict avoidance of phytanic acid sources (dairy fat, fatty fish, ruminant meat)
Ketogenic diet to promote fatty acid oxidation
Low-phenylalanine diet
questions.explanation: Refsum disease (PHYH or PEX7 mutations) causes accumulation of phytanic acid — a branched-chain fatty acid from dietary chlorophyll-containing foods. Since humans cannot catabolize phytanic acid without the peroxisomal enzyme phytanoyl-CoA hydroxylase, strict dietary elimination of sources (dairy fat, fatty fish, beef/lamb) can halt or reverse some symptoms. Plasmapheresis is used acutely for rapid phytanic acid reduction. The 5-feature syndrome (retinitis pigmentosa + ataxia + neuropathy + anosmia + ichthyosis) is diagnostic.

questions.question: A 7-year-old boy has a demyelinating neuropathy (MCV 15 m/s) with onset of foot drop at age 2. He has scoliosis, distal wasting, and onion-bulb formation on nerve biopsy. His parents are unaffected. What type of mutation is most likely?
choices: Inherited PMP22 duplication from an affected parent
De novo dominant mutation in PMP22, MPZ, or EGR2 causing Dejerine-Sottas disease
Autosomal recessive GDAP1 mutation
X-linked connexin-32 (GJB1) mutation
questions.explanation: Dejerine-Sottas disease (DSS) / congenital hypomyelinating neuropathy presents with severe early-onset demyelinating neuropathy (often MCV <10–15 m/s), scoliosis, and marked nerve hypertrophy with onion bulbs. Most cases are caused by de novo dominant mutations in PMP22, MPZ, or EGR2 — parents are typically unaffected. This distinguishes DSS from CMT1A (inherited from affected parent) and from HMSNIII. Genetic testing of parents (both negative) combined with extreme slowing and severe early onset is the clinical clue.

questions.question: A 35-year-old woman develops diplopia, ptosis, and symmetric ascending weakness 4 weeks after Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis. Her reflexes are absent. Cerebellar ataxia is prominent. Anti-GQ1b IgG antibodies are positive at high titer. The most likely diagnosis is:
choices: AIDP (Guillain-Barré syndrome, classic form)
Miller Fisher syndrome (MFS) — a GBS variant
Bickerstaff brainstem encephalitis
Myasthenia gravis (anti-AChR positive)
questions.explanation: Miller Fisher syndrome (MFS) is defined by the triad of ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, and areflexia. Anti-GQ1b IgG antibodies are present in >90% of cases and are essentially diagnostic. MFS is considered a GBS variant with a generally favorable prognosis; most patients recover fully within weeks to months without specific treatment (IVIG can be given if severe). Bickerstaff brainstem encephalitis overlaps with MFS but includes altered consciousness and is associated with MRI brainstem changes. Campylobacter is the most common preceding infection for GBS/MFS.

questions.question: A 34-year-old woman has ptosis, diplopia, and fatigable proximal limb weakness. Serum acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies are negative. Single-fiber EMG shows increased jitter and blocking. She responds partially to pyridostigmine. What is the next most useful diagnostic test?
choices: Tensilon (edrophonium) test
Anti-MuSK and anti-LRP4 antibody testing
Chest CT for thymoma
Repetitive nerve stimulation at 3 Hz
questions.explanation: In seronegative MG (AChR antibody negative), the next priority is testing for anti-MuSK antibodies (~40–50% of seronegative generalized MG) and anti-LRP4 antibodies (~10%). These define distinct clinical subtypes with different treatment approaches: MuSK-MG responds poorly to pyridostigmine and thymectomy is not beneficial; rituximab is often effective. True seronegative MG (negative for all three antibodies) exists but is a diagnosis of exclusion. SFEMG is already the most sensitive diagnostic test and has been done.

questions.question: A 48-year-old man with generalized AChR-positive MG is evaluated for thymectomy. CT shows no thymoma. He has been on pyridostigmine and prednisone for 2 years with incomplete control. What does the MGTX trial (Wolfe et al., 2016) indicate about thymectomy in non-thymomatous MG?
choices: Thymectomy provides no benefit in non-thymomatous MG
Extended transsternal thymectomy improves clinical outcomes and reduces steroid requirements in AChR-positive MG aged 18–65
Thymectomy is only beneficial if performed within 1 year of diagnosis
Thymectomy is equivalent to medical management for MuSK-positive MG
questions.explanation: The MGTX RCT (NEJM 2016) demonstrated that extended transsternal thymectomy significantly improved QMG scores and reduced prednisone requirements and hospitalization rates in AChR-positive, non-thymomatous MG patients aged 18–65. This established thymectomy as standard of care for this group — a change from prior uncertainty. Thymectomy is NOT beneficial for MuSK-MG or late-onset MG in some guidelines. Robotic/thoracoscopic approaches are now used in many centers.

questions.question: A 60-year-old male smoker develops bilateral ptosis, proximal limb weakness worse in the legs, and dry mouth. Strength improves briefly after repeated contractions (post-exercise facilitation). 3 Hz repetitive nerve stimulation shows 15% decrement, and 50 Hz stimulation shows 200% increment. Chest CT shows a hilar mass. What is the pathophysiology?
choices: Post-synaptic AChR blockade by IgG antibodies (myasthenia gravis)
Presynaptic impairment of acetylcholine vesicle release due to VGCC antibodies (Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome)
Presynaptic toxin cleavage of SNARE proteins (botulism)
Absent acetylcholinesterase at the NMJ (COLQ-CMS)
questions.explanation: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) is a presynaptic NMJ disorder caused by IgG antibodies against voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCC P/Q-type). Reduced Ca²⁺ influx impairs ACh vesicle fusion, causing proximal weakness (legs > arms), hyporeflexia, autonomic features, and post-exercise facilitation. >100% increment on high-frequency RNS is pathognomonic. ~60% of LEMS is paraneoplastic (SCLC most common). Treatment: 3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) symptomatically; treat underlying malignancy.

questions.question: A 5-year-old boy presents with fatigable ptosis, hypotonia, and feeding difficulties since infancy. AChR and MuSK antibodies are negative. His twin sister is unaffected. Repetitive nerve stimulation shows a decremental response. Genetic testing identifies compound heterozygous mutations in DOK7. Pyridostigmine makes him worse. What treatment is appropriate?
choices: Increase pyridostigmine to higher doses
Albuterol (salbutamol) or ephedrine — beta-adrenergic agonists
Rituximab for seronegative autoimmune MG
3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine)
questions.explanation: DOK7 congenital myasthenic syndrome (DOK7-CMS) is caused by recessive mutations in the DOK7 adaptor protein, which is essential for MuSK signaling and NMJ development. It presents with limb-girdle predominant weakness, ptosis, and fatigue — without response to (or worsening with) pyridostigmine. Beta-2 adrenergic agonists (albuterol/salbutamol, ephedrine) are the effective treatment, promoting NMJ maturation and AChR clustering. This is the opposite of autoimmune MG treatment, and the distinction is a critical clinical point.

questions.question: A 3-month-old infant presents with descending paralysis: first poor feeding and weak cry, then loss of head control, then arm weakness. Pupils are poorly reactive (dilated). He has no fever. His mother fed him honey in warm tea 2 weeks ago. Stool culture grows Clostridium botulinum. What is the mechanism of toxin action?
choices: Post-synaptic ACh receptor blockade
Presynaptic cleavage of SNARE complex proteins (SNAP-25, VAMP/synaptobrevin, syntaxin), preventing ACh vesicle fusion
Presynaptic VGCC antibody blockade (as in LEMS)
Inhibition of acetylcholinesterase at the NMJ
questions.explanation: Botulinum toxin (7 serotypes) is a zinc-endopeptidase that cleaves presynaptic SNARE complex proteins (SNAP-25 for type A/E, VAMP/synaptobrevin for type B/D/F, syntaxin for type C) — preventing ACh vesicle docking and fusion, causing flaccid paralysis. Honey contains C. botulinum spores that colonize the infant intestine. Key distinguishing features from MG: descending paralysis (not ascending as in GBS), pupil involvement (mydriasis), no fever, normal sensory exam, autonomic features. Treatment: BabyBIG (botulism immune globulin) in infants.

questions.question: A 2-week-old term neonate has difficulty feeding, weak cry, and hypotonia. Her mother has known myasthenia gravis (AChR antibody positive) managed with pyridostigmine. The neonate's AChR antibodies are positive. What is the expected course?
choices: This is congenital myasthenic syndrome — genetic, lifelong, requires chronic pyridostigmine
This is transient neonatal myasthenia due to passive maternal AChR antibody transfer — resolves spontaneously in 2–8 weeks
This is congenital AChR deficiency — treat with 3,4-DAP
This requires thymectomy in the first year of life
questions.explanation: Transient neonatal myasthenia occurs in ~10–15% of infants born to mothers with AChR-positive MG, due to passive placental transfer of maternal IgG AChR antibodies. It is self-resolving as maternal antibodies are catabolized over 2–8 weeks. Supportive care ± pyridostigmine may be needed acutely. This is completely distinct from congenital myasthenic syndromes (genetic, non-autoimmune, lifelong). The neonatal AChR antibodies originate from the mother, not from the infant's own immune system.

questions.question: A 22-year-old woman has a 3-year history of myoclonic jerks, progressive cerebellar ataxia, and generalized seizures. Her maternal grandmother had deafness and diabetes. Muscle biopsy shows ragged-red fibers (RRF) on Gomori trichrome and succinate dehydrogenase-positive, COX-negative fibers. Which mtDNA mutation is most likely?
choices: m.3243A>G (tRNA-Leu — MELAS)
m.8344A>G (tRNA-Lys — MERRF)
Large-scale mtDNA deletion (Kearns-Sayre/CPEO)
m.11778G>A (ND4 — Leber hereditary optic neuropathy)
questions.explanation: MERRF (Myoclonic Epilepsy with Ragged-Red Fibers) is most commonly caused by m.8344A>G in the mitochondrial tRNALys gene. The clinical hallmarks are myoclonus, ataxia, seizures, and RRF on muscle biopsy. Hearing loss, dementia, and peripheral neuropathy are variable. Maternal inheritance pattern (grandmother with deafness/diabetes suggesting mitochondrial disease) supports the diagnosis. m.3243A>G is the MELAS mutation. Large-scale deletions cause KSS. LHON causes only optic neuropathy.

questions.question: A 14-month-old presents with hypotonia, developmental regression, and feeding difficulties after a viral illness. MRI shows bilateral symmetric T2 hyperintensities in the basal ganglia and brainstem. Plasma lactate is 5.2 mmol/L. What syndrome best fits, and what gene family is most commonly implicated?
choices: MELAS — mitochondrial tRNA mutations
Leigh syndrome — SURF1, complex I subunit genes, or POLG (dozens of nuclear-encoded genes)
Kearns-Sayre syndrome — large-scale mtDNA deletion
MERRF — m.8344A>G
questions.explanation: Leigh syndrome (subacute necrotizing encephalomyelopathy) presents in infancy–early childhood with developmental regression after illness, hypotonia, and bilateral symmetric MRI lesions in basal ganglia/brainstem. Elevated lactate reflects oxidative phosphorylation failure. Genetically heterogeneous: SURF1 (Complex IV assembly) is most commonly identified, but mutations in >75 genes (Complex I subunits, POLG, mtDNA-encoded genes) cause the Leigh phenotype. Mitochondrial DNA whole-genome sequencing plus nuclear gene panels are needed for complete workup.

questions.question: A 16-year-old boy has bilateral ptosis and progressive external ophthalmoplegia since age 12, pigmentary retinopathy, and a recent ECG showing complete heart block. His CK is mildly elevated. Muscle biopsy shows RRF and COX-negative fibers. A large-scale mtDNA deletion is confirmed on Southern blot. What is the diagnosis, and what is the most urgent intervention?
choices: CPEO with RRF — observation and annual cardiology review
Kearns-Sayre syndrome — cardiac pacing for complete heart block (risk of sudden death)
MELAS — initiate L-arginine supplementation
MERRF — optimize seizure management with valproate
questions.explanation: Kearns-Sayre syndrome (KSS) is defined by: onset <20 years, CPEO, and pigmentary retinopathy, PLUS at least one of: cardiac conduction block, CSF protein >100 mg/dL, or cerebellar ataxia. All caused by large-scale single mtDNA deletions. Cardiac conduction block (even first-degree AV block) requires prompt cardiology referral — complete heart block in KSS can cause sudden cardiac death and permanent pacing is often indicated. Valproate should be avoided in mitochondrial disease (hepatotoxicity risk).

questions.question: A 28-year-old man has painless bilateral, sequential visual loss over 2 months (right eye first, then left eye 8 weeks later). Central scotoma. He smokes heavily. His maternal cousin had the same presentation. No other neurological findings. Fundoscopy shows circumpapillary telangiectasia. Which mutation is responsible in ~70% of cases?
choices: m.3243A>G (tRNA-Leu)
m.11778G>A (ND4 subunit of Complex I — LHON)
m.8344A>G (tRNA-Lys)
Large-scale mtDNA deletion
questions.explanation: Leber hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON) causes painless, bilateral (usually sequential) optic neuropathy in young adults, predominantly males. The m.11778G>A mutation (ND4) accounts for ~70% of LHON globally; m.3460G>A and m.14484T>C are the other primary mutations. Maternal inheritance and male predominance (incomplete penetrance, with environmental modifiers like smoking). Idebenone (CoQ10 analog) is an FDA-approved treatment for LHON. The circumpapillary telangiectasia is an acute-phase fundoscopic finding.

questions.question: A 4-year-old girl presents with intractable seizures, developmental regression, and hepatic dysfunction. Muscle biopsy shows mtDNA depletion. Her mother is asymptomatic. Sequencing reveals compound heterozygous POLG mutations. Valproate was started for seizures 2 months ago. What is the most urgent action?
choices: Increase valproate dose for better seizure control
Discontinue valproate immediately — it is contraindicated in POLG disease (fatal hepatotoxicity)
Add levetiracetam to valproate for combination therapy
Initiate coenzyme Q10 supplementation and continue valproate
questions.explanation: POLG-related disorders (including Alpers-Huttenlocher syndrome) are caused by recessive POLG mutations and cause mtDNA depletion/deletion in liver and brain. Valproate is absolutely contraindicated in POLG disease: it causes severe, often fatal hepatotoxicity in this context, likely by further impairing mitochondrial function. This is a life-threatening drug interaction. Alternative anticonvulsants (levetiracetam, phenobarbital, clonazepam) should be used. This contraindication applies to any suspected mitochondrial disease until genetic testing is complete.

questions.question: A mother asks whether her mitochondrial disorder can be passed to her son. She carries a pathogenic m.3243A>G mutation with 40% heteroplasmy in blood. What is the most accurate counseling statement?
choices: Her son has a 50% chance of inheriting the mutation, following Mendelian autosomal dominant rules
Mitochondrial DNA is maternally inherited; all of her children are at risk, but the level of heteroplasmy and resulting disease severity are unpredictable due to mitotic segregation
Her son cannot be affected since MELAS predominantly affects females
If the mutation is not detected in her son's blood at birth, he will remain unaffected
questions.explanation: MtDNA follows strict maternal inheritance — all children of an affected mother are at risk regardless of sex. However, the proportion of mutant mtDNA (heteroplasmy) varies dramatically between tissues and between generations due to mitotic segregation and a mitochondrial genetic bottleneck during oogenesis. Blood heteroplasmy may not reflect muscle or CNS heteroplasmy. Severity and age of onset cannot be predicted from blood heteroplasmy alone. This unpredictability makes prenatal counseling complex; preimplantation genetic testing and mitochondrial donation (in licensed centers) are options.

questions.question: A 15-year-old girl has progressive gait ataxia, areflexia, dysarthria, and cardiomyopathy. Genetic testing shows homozygous GAA trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FXN gene (1,200 repeats on both alleles). What is the mechanism of pathology?
choices: Toxic gain-of-function from the expanded GAA repeat RNA
Epigenetic silencing of FXN by the GAA repeat, reducing frataxin protein and causing mitochondrial iron accumulation
Frataxin protein misfolding causing toxic aggregates in Purkinje cells
Loss of frataxin destabilizes coenzyme Q10 synthesis
questions.explanation: Friedreich ataxia is caused by autosomal recessive GAA repeat expansion in intron 1 of FXN. The repeat expansion causes heterochromatin formation (epigenetic silencing) that suppresses FXN transcription, reducing frataxin protein. Frataxin is essential for iron-sulfur cluster assembly in mitochondria; its loss causes mitochondrial iron accumulation, oxidative stress, and neurodegeneration — predominantly of dorsal root ganglia, spinocerebellar tracts, and corticospinal tracts. Cardiomyopathy affects ~80% of patients and is the leading cause of death.

questions.question: A 17-year-old girl with Friedreich ataxia is found to have progressive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy on echo. She has been following a clinical trial. The FDA approved omaveloxolone (Skyclarys) for Friedreich ataxia in 2023. What is its mechanism?
choices: It replaces frataxin protein directly via enzyme replacement
It activates the Nrf2 pathway, enhancing antioxidant response and reducing mitochondrial oxidative stress
It is an iron chelator removing excess mitochondrial iron
It is an antisense oligonucleotide reducing the GAA repeat expansion
questions.explanation: Omaveloxolone (Skyclarys, FDA 2023) is the first approved treatment for Friedreich ataxia. It is a semisynthetic triterpenoid that activates the Nrf2 transcription factor, upregulating antioxidant response element genes, improving mitochondrial function, and reducing reactive oxygen species. It does not restore frataxin levels directly. The pivotal MOXIe trial showed slowing of neurological decline on the modified FA Rating Scale. It is indicated for patients aged ≥16 years. Cardiac management follows standard hypertrophic cardiomyopathy guidelines.

questions.question: A 9-year-old girl has cerebellar ataxia, oculomotor apraxia, and bilateral conjunctival telangiectasias. She has had 3 sinopulmonary infections this year. Serum AFP is elevated. Lymphocyte count is low and immunoglobulins are reduced. Which gene is mutated?
choices: FXN (frataxin — Friedreich ataxia)
ATM (ataxia-telangiectasia mutated kinase)
APTX (aprataxin — ataxia with oculomotor apraxia type 1)
SETX (senataxin — AOA type 2)
questions.explanation: Ataxia-telangiectasia (AT) is caused by biallelic loss-of-function mutations in ATM, encoding a DNA damage-response kinase. The classic features are cerebellar ataxia (onset age 1–4), oculomotor apraxia, conjunctival/skin telangiectasias, combined immunodeficiency (IgA/IgG deficiency, lymphopenia), markedly elevated serum AFP, and extreme radiosensitivity. Patients have ~100-fold increased cancer risk (lymphomas, leukemias). AFP elevation (a unique biomarker in AT) helps distinguish AT from other early-onset ataxias. Radiation and radiomimetic drugs must be avoided.

questions.question: A 40-year-old man has slowly progressive gait ataxia, dysarthria, and dysphagia. He also has spasticity and dystonia. His father and grandfather were similarly affected, with onset in their 30s. Genetic testing reveals CAG repeat expansion in ATXN3 (46 repeats). What is the most common worldwide spinocerebellar ataxia?
choices: SCA1 (ATXN1)
SCA2 (ATXN2)
SCA3 — Machado-Joseph disease (ATXN3)
SCA6 (CACNA1A)
questions.explanation: SCA3 (Machado-Joseph disease, caused by CAG repeat in ATXN3) is the most common autosomal dominant spinocerebellar ataxia worldwide, particularly prevalent in populations of Portuguese/Azorean descent. Clinical features include cerebellar ataxia, pyramidal signs, extrapyramidal features, peripheral neuropathy, and progressive gaze palsy. The polyglutamine-expanded ataxin-3 forms nuclear aggregates in neurons. Anticipation occurs (earlier onset with more repeats in subsequent generations). Normal ATXN3 repeat <44; disease ≥61.

questions.question: A 24-year-old woman has progressive ataxia with clinical features identical to Friedreich ataxia: areflexia, dorsal column sensory loss, and cardiomyopathy. However, frataxin levels are normal and no GAA repeat expansion is found. Her serum vitamin E is undetectable. What is the diagnosis and treatment?
choices: Spinocerebellar ataxia type 1 (SCA1 — no treatment)
Ataxia with vitamin E deficiency (AVED — TTPA mutation) — high-dose vitamin E supplementation
Ataxia-telangiectasia (ATM) — supportive care only
Refsum disease (PHYH) — dietary restriction
questions.explanation: Ataxia with vitamin E deficiency (AVED) is caused by autosomal recessive mutations in TTPA (tocopherol transfer protein), impairing hepatic incorporation of alpha-tocopherol into VLDL. The phenotype is clinically indistinguishable from Friedreich ataxia (FA-like, with cardiomyopathy, areflexia, and dorsal column signs) but frataxin levels are normal. Critically, AVED is one of the few treatable ataxias: high-dose oral vitamin E supplementation stabilizes or reverses neurological symptoms. Measuring serum vitamin E in all FA-like presentations is essential to avoid missing this treatable cause.

resource.label: GeneReviews: Ataxia with Vitamin E Deficiency

questions.question: A 22-year-old man develops severe myalgia and dark urine (myoglobinuria) after a 20-mile hiking trip. He has had similar milder episodes after prolonged fasting. CK peaks at 85,000 IU/L. At rest he is completely normal. Ischemic forearm exercise test shows appropriate lactate AND ammonia rise. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: McArdle disease (PYGM) — no lactate rise on exercise test
Carnitine palmitoyltransferase II deficiency (CPT2 — long-chain fatty acid oxidation disorder)
Very-long-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (VLCAD)
Phosphofructokinase deficiency (Tarui disease)
questions.explanation: CPT2 deficiency is the most common cause of recurrent exercise-induced myoglobinuria in adults. Triggers are prolonged low-intensity exercise (fatty acid-dependent), fasting, fever, and cold — unlike McArdle disease (brief intense exercise triggers, 'second wind'). The ischemic forearm exercise test is NORMAL in CPT2 (glycolysis is intact — normal lactate rise), distinguishing it from glycogenolytic/glycolytic defects. Between episodes CK normalizes and strength is normal. The CPT2 enzyme controls mitochondrial entry of long-chain fatty acids as acylcarnitines.

questions.question: A 12-year-old boy complains of muscle stiffness when starting to move (e.g., first few steps after sitting). Stiffness improves with continued movement ('warm-up phenomenon'). No weakness. CK is 350 IU/L. His older sister has the same complaint but milder. Nerve conduction is normal. EMG shows myotonic discharges without clinical myotonia on exam. Which gene and inheritance pattern?
choices: SCN4A (Nav1.4) — autosomal dominant (paramyotonia congenita)
CLCN1 (chloride channel) — autosomal dominant (Thomsen disease) or recessive (Becker myotonia)
DMPK (CTG repeat) — autosomal dominant (myotonic dystrophy type 1)
KCNJ2 (Kir2.1) — autosomal dominant (Andersen-Tawil syndrome)
questions.explanation: Myotonia congenita is caused by mutations in CLCN1 (muscle chloride channel). Loss of chloride channel function prolongs membrane repolarization, causing repetitive action potentials (myotonia). Two forms: Thomsen disease (AD, milder) and Becker myotonia (AR, more severe — transient weakness on initiation of movement). Both show warm-up phenomenon (stiffness improves with repeated contractions), normal strength, and no systemic involvement. Mexiletine is the first-line pharmacotherapy for symptomatic myotonia. DM1 (DMPK) has systemic features and distal weakness.

questions.question: A 20-year-old man develops pronounced facial stiffness after eating ice cream, and muscle stiffness in his hands that worsens (not improves) with repeated contractions in cold weather. No weakness at rest. His father has identical cold-triggered stiffness. Nerve conduction and EMG are performed in a warm room and are normal. What is the diagnosis?
choices: Myotonia congenita (CLCN1) — warm-up phenomenon expected
Paramyotonia congenita (SCN4A) — paradoxical worsening of myotonia with exercise and cold
Hyperkalemic periodic paralysis (SCN4A) — myotonia with cold-triggered paralysis
Schwartz-Jampel syndrome (HSPG2) — continuous myotonia and skeletal dysplasia
questions.explanation: Paramyotonia congenita (PMC) is caused by dominant SCN4A (Nav1.4) mutations and is distinguished by paradoxical myotonia — stiffness that WORSENS with repeated contractions (opposite of the warm-up phenomenon in CLCN1 myotonia). Cold is the classic trigger; even eating cold food triggers facial stiffness. Attacks can progress to flaccid weakness. EMG done at room temperature may be normal; cold challenge provokes myotonic discharges. Mexiletine is effective. PMC and hyperkalemic PP are allelic SCN4A disorders; some patients have both.

questions.question: A 14-year-old boy presents with episodic flaccid limb weakness lasting hours, associated with bidirectional ventricular tachycardia on ECG (QTc 490 ms). He has hypertelorism, low-set ears, and small mandible. Between episodes he is neurologically normal. What is the diagnosis?
choices: Hypokalemic periodic paralysis type 1 (CACNA1S)
Andersen-Tawil syndrome (KCNJ2 — triad of periodic paralysis + cardiac arrhythmia + dysmorphic features)
Hyperkalemic periodic paralysis (SCN4A)
Timothy syndrome (CACNA1C — QT prolongation + autism + syndactyly)
questions.explanation: Andersen-Tawil syndrome (ATS) is caused by dominant KCNJ2 mutations (Kir2.1 inward-rectifier K⁺ channel) and is defined by the triad: (1) episodic flaccid weakness (periodic paralysis, usually hypokalemic), (2) cardiac arrhythmia (bidirectional VT, QTc prolongation, PVCs), and (3) dysmorphic features (hypertelorism, micrognathia, low-set ears, clinodactyly, small hands/feet). Not all three features need be present simultaneously. The cardiac arrhythmia can be life-threatening; ICD implantation may be required. Acetazolamide and potassium supplementation reduce paralytic attacks.

questions.question: A 17-year-old boy presents with exercise-induced stiffness that is electrically SILENT on EMG (no myotonic discharges). After exercise his muscles feel 'locked' but relax with warmth. CK is normal. His maternal uncle had similar symptoms. What is the diagnosis?
choices: Myotonia congenita (CLCN1) — myotonic discharges always present on EMG
Brody disease (ATP2A1 — SERCA1 deficiency) — electrically silent contracture
Paramyotonia congenita (SCN4A) — cold-triggered with worsening on exercise
Rippling muscle disease (CAV3) — ripples visible on percussion
questions.explanation: Brody disease is caused by autosomal recessive ATP2A1 mutations (SERCA1, the sarcoplasmic/endoplasmic reticulum Ca²⁺-ATPase of fast-twitch muscle). Impaired Ca²⁺ re-uptake after contraction delays relaxation, causing exercise-induced stiffness — but crucially, the muscle is electrically silent on EMG (no myotonic discharges), distinguishing it from all myotonic disorders. The 'contracture' is biochemical, not electrical. Rare; AD form with partial SERCA1 deficiency also described. No approved treatment; calcium channel blockers and dantrolene may help symptomatically.

questions.question: A 16-year-old boy has a triad of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, proximal myopathy (CK 2,500 IU/L), and mild intellectual disability. His maternal grandfather had a pacemaker and similar muscle weakness. Muscle biopsy shows vacuolar myopathy with lysosomal glycogen accumulation but NORMAL acid alpha-glucosidase enzyme activity. What is the diagnosis?
choices: Pompe disease (GAA deficiency) — enzyme activity would be low
Danon disease (LAMP2 — X-linked) — normal GAA activity, autophagosome accumulation
GSD type III (GSD-III, debranching enzyme) — liver + muscle, usually AR
GSD type IV (branching enzyme) — rare, liver-predominant
questions.explanation: Danon disease is caused by X-linked mutations in LAMP2 (lysosome-associated membrane protein 2). The clinical triad is hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (severe, often requiring transplant), skeletal myopathy with elevated CK, and intellectual disability (variable). Muscle biopsy shows vacuolar myopathy with autophagic vacuoles and glycogen accumulation — but GAA enzyme activity is NORMAL (unlike Pompe). Maternal grandfather transmission is consistent with X-linked. Cardiac disease is life-threatening, often requiring ICD or transplant. No approved disease-modifying therapy exists.

questions.question: A 30-year-old woman with generalized myalgia is found to have CK of 3,200 IU/L on routine labs. She started a new medication 6 weeks ago. She has no weakness on exam. Her medication is most likely which of the following?
choices: Metformin (biguanide)
High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80 mg)
ACE inhibitor
Beta-blocker
questions.explanation: Statin-induced myopathy is the most common drug-induced muscle disease. High-intensity statins (atorvastatin 40–80 mg, rosuvastatin 20–40 mg) carry the highest myotoxicity risk. Spectrum: myalgia alone (most common), myositis with CK elevation, and rare rhabdomyolysis. Risk factors: high dose, drug interactions (CYP3A4 inhibitors like azithromycin, gemfibrozil), hypothyroidism, renal/hepatic disease. Anti-HMGCR antibodies indicate immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (IMNM) — a rare but serious statin-triggered autoimmune condition requiring immunosuppression even after statin discontinuation.

questions.question: You are performing EMG on a 55-year-old man with proximal leg weakness. Needle electrode shows spontaneous activity (fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves) in the resting muscle. What does this finding indicate?
choices: This is a normal finding in resting skeletal muscle — no significance
Active denervation OR active myopathy with muscle fiber necrosis — indicating ongoing muscle membrane instability
This is diagnostic for ALS only and requires urgent referral
Fibrillation potentials are only seen in radiculopathy, not myopathy
questions.explanation: Fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves (spontaneous activity in resting muscle) represent membrane instability of individual muscle fibers. They occur in: (1) denervation (axon loss from any cause — neuropathy, radiculopathy, anterior horn cell disease), and (2) active myopathy with fiber necrosis (inflammatory myopathies, muscular dystrophies — the necrotic fibers lose the influence of motor nerve terminals). Their presence narrows the differential to conditions with ongoing fiber damage; their distribution (proximal vs. distal, paraspinal vs. limb) guides further diagnosis. They do NOT occur in normal muscle.

questions.question: A 10-year-old boy presents to the emergency department with dark urine after a febrile illness. CK is 45,000 IU/L. Urinalysis shows large blood without RBCs on microscopy (myoglobinuria). Creatinine is rising. Which intervention is most important to prevent acute kidney injury?
choices: Furosemide diuresis to concentrate urine
Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with isotonic saline to maintain urine output >3 mL/kg/hr; consider urinary alkalinization
Sodium bicarbonate alone without IV fluids
Emergent dialysis for all CK >10,000
questions.explanation: Myoglobin precipitates in renal tubules (especially in acidic concentrated urine), causing acute tubular necrosis. The cornerstone of rhabdomyolysis management is aggressive IV normal saline hydration targeting urine output >200–300 mL/hr (or 3 mL/kg/hr in children) to flush myoglobin. Urinary alkalinization (sodium bicarbonate added to IV fluids) reduces myoglobin precipitation and may be beneficial, though evidence is less robust. Furosemide can worsen tubular concentration and acidification. Dialysis is reserved for refractory hyperkalemia or renal failure — not routine at any CK threshold.

questions.question: A 60-year-old man with rapidly progressive symmetric proximal weakness has a CK of 6,000 IU/L. EMG shows active myopathic changes with fibrillations. Muscle biopsy shows necrotic and regenerating fibers with sparse inflammation and MHC-I upregulation. Anti-HMGCR antibodies are positive. He took atorvastatin for 5 years. He stopped 2 months ago but is getting worse. What is the diagnosis and treatment?
choices: Statin myopathy — resolves with statin discontinuation alone
Immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (IMNM) triggered by statin — requires immunosuppressive therapy despite statin cessation
Polymyositis — treat with high-dose prednisone
Sporadic inclusion body myositis — no effective immunotherapy
questions.explanation: Anti-HMGCR immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (IMNM) is a statin-associated autoimmune myopathy in which statins trigger an autoimmune response targeting HMGCR (the statin's target enzyme). Unlike simple statin myotoxicity, IMNM does NOT resolve with statin cessation — it is a true autoimmune disease requiring immunosuppression (prednisone plus IVIG and/or steroid-sparing agents like azathioprine or rituximab). Biopsy shows necrosis without significant inflammation (distinguishing it from polymyositis). Anti-HMGCR antibody titer correlates with disease activity.

questions.question: A 12-year-old girl has slowly progressive proximal weakness. EMG shows myopathic features. Muscle biopsy shows numerous 'rimmed vacuoles' in >20% of fibers and filamentous inclusions on electron microscopy. There is no inflammation. CK is 800 IU/L. Genetic testing identifies compound heterozygous GNE mutations. What is the diagnosis?
choices: Sporadic inclusion body myositis (sIBM) — acquired, age >50
GNE myopathy (hereditary inclusion body myopathy / Nonaka myopathy) — distal > proximal, spares quadriceps
VCP-related myopathy (multisystem proteinopathy)
LGMD R3 (alpha-sarcoglycanopathy)
questions.explanation: GNE myopathy (formerly HIBM / Nonaka myopathy) is caused by recessive GNE mutations affecting UDP-GlcNAc epimerase (sialic acid biosynthesis). It presents in young adults (teens–30s) with distal > proximal weakness, characteristically SPARING the quadriceps even in advanced disease. Rimmed vacuoles on biopsy are the hallmark, with TDP-43 and IBM-style inclusions. This is genetically determined, not autoimmune — immunosuppression is ineffective. Aceneuramic acid (sialic acid) supplementation is in clinical trials. Distinguished from sIBM by young age and AR inheritance.

questions.question: A 6-year-old boy with SMA type 1 is on nusinersen (started at age 4). His parents ask about switching to onasemnogene abeparvovec. Which statement is most accurate?
choices: Onasemnogene abeparvovec can be given at any age and replaces nusinersen permanently
Onasemnogene abeparvovec is currently approved up to age 2 (or weight limit 21 kg); at age 6 he would not qualify based on current label
Switching is contraindicated because prior AAV exposure from onasemnogene causes immune reactions
Nusinersen and onasemnogene can be safely combined for additive benefit
questions.explanation: Onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma) is FDA-approved for SMA patients <2 years of age (US label), based on the AVXS-101 trials showing the most dramatic benefit in very young/pre-symptomatic patients. At age 6, this boy exceeds the approved age range. Nusinersen (intrathecal ASO) and risdiplam (oral) remain appropriate long-term options across all ages. Pre-existing anti-AAV9 antibodies are an exclusion criterion for onasemnogene. Combination therapy is being studied but is not standard of care.

questions.question: A 40-year-old woman presents with subacute proximal weakness, a heliotrope rash, Gottron's papules, and V-sign erythema. CK is 2,800 IU/L. Chest CT shows ground-glass opacities. Myositis antibody panel shows anti-MDA5 positivity. What is the most critical concern in her management?
choices: High risk of thymoma — obtain chest CT
Rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease (ILD) — a potentially fatal complication of anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis
High risk of cardiac arrhythmia — initiate continuous telemetry
Rhabdomyolysis — aggressive hydration required
questions.explanation: Anti-MDA5 (melanoma differentiation-associated gene 5) antibody-positive dermatomyositis is associated with clinically amyopathic DM and rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease (RP-ILD), which carries a high mortality (30–50% within 6 months if untreated). This is the most urgent concern — ILD progression can be rapid even when muscle disease is mild. Early aggressive immunosuppression (high-dose steroids + calcineurin inhibitor + IVIG or rituximab) is required. ILD surveillance with HRCT and pulmonary function tests is mandatory at diagnosis.

questions.question: An 8-year-old girl with a progressive myopathy has a muscle biopsy showing lipid droplet accumulation in type 1 fibers on oil red O stain, with normal acid alpha-glucosidase activity and normal CPT2 sequence. She has mild proximal weakness and responds dramatically to riboflavin supplementation. Which gene is most likely?
choices: ETFDH (electron transfer flavoprotein dehydrogenase) — riboflavin-responsive MADD
CPT2 (carnitine palmitoyltransferase II)
ACADL (long-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase)
GAA (Pompe disease)
questions.explanation: Riboflavin-responsive multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (MADD/glutaric aciduria type II) due to ETFDH mutations presents with lipid storage myopathy, proximal weakness, and dramatic response to riboflavin (vitamin B2). ETFDH encodes a component of the electron transfer chain that requires FAD (riboflavin-derived). Lipid droplets accumulate when fatty acid beta-oxidation is impaired. Riboflavin response distinguishes this from other lipid myopathies. Riboflavin + CoQ10 supplementation is the treatment. Urinary organic acids show multiple acylcarnitine elevations on acylcarnitine profile.

questions.question: A 55-year-old man presents with acute onset of severe unilateral shoulder and periscapular pain, followed within days by rapid proximal arm weakness and winging of the scapula. CK is 400 IU/L. EMG shows active denervation in a non-dermatomal pattern affecting the suprascapular and long thoracic nerves. MRI brachial plexus shows no mass. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: Cervical radiculopathy (C5–C6)
Neuralgic amyotrophy (Parsonage-Turner syndrome / idiopathic brachial plexopathy)
Multifocal motor neuropathy
LGMD affecting shoulder-girdle muscles
questions.explanation: Neuralgic amyotrophy (Parsonage-Turner syndrome) presents with acute severe shoulder pain followed by rapid weakness and atrophy in the brachial plexus distribution — often affecting individual named nerves (suprascapular, long thoracic, anterior interosseous). EMG confirms active denervation in a multifocal, non-dermatomal pattern. Idiopathic form (most common) is thought to be immune-mediated, often triggered by infection, surgery, or vaccination. Hereditary NA (SEPT9 mutations) is clinically identical but recurrent. Most patients recover substantially over 1–3 years; high-dose steroids may shorten the painful phase.

questions.question: A 3-year-old girl has recurrent episodes of hypoglycemia and hypotonia during illness or fasting. Liver is enlarged. Between episodes she has mild proximal weakness. Biopsy of liver shows PAS-positive glycogen and reduced debranching enzyme activity. Which GSD subtype is this?
choices: GSD type I (glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency — von Gierke disease)
GSD type III (amylo-1,6-glucosidase / debranching enzyme deficiency — Cori/Forbes disease)
GSD type V (muscle phosphorylase / McArdle disease)
GSD type II (acid alpha-glucosidase / Pompe disease)
questions.explanation: GSD type III (Cori/Forbes disease) is caused by amylo-1,6-glucosidase (debranching enzyme, AGL gene) deficiency. Glycogen accumulates with shortened outer chains in liver and/or muscle. Clinical features: hepatomegaly, hypoglycemia with fasting, and variable muscle involvement (myopathy + elevated CK). Unlike GSD I, fasting hypoglycemia is less severe (amino acid gluconeogenesis is intact). Unlike McArdle disease (GSD V), liver is also involved. Diagnosis: enzyme assay in blood or muscle, AGL gene sequencing. Management: frequent feeding, high-protein diet.

questions.question: A 28-year-old man with SMA type 3 (walks independently, mild proximal weakness) asks about monitoring and preventive care. Which complication requires specific surveillance?
choices: Cardiac arrhythmia — annual Holter monitoring
Respiratory failure and scoliosis — spirometry and spine imaging annually
Renal impairment — annual renal function panel
Hepatotoxicity from nusinersen — annual LFTs
questions.explanation: All SMA types require respiratory monitoring (spirometry, especially supine FVC) because diaphragmatic weakness can develop insidiously — even in ambulatory type 3 patients. Scoliosis is common and progressive, particularly with prolonged sitting. Annual respiratory function testing and spine surveillance with X-ray are standard. Nusinersen is intrathecally delivered and does not require hepatic monitoring. Cardiac arrhythmia is NOT a primary SMA complication (unlike DMD or Emery-Dreifuss MD). The respiratory and musculoskeletal complications drive morbidity in SMA.

questions.question: A 45-year-old man has slowly progressive, symmetric proximal and distal weakness. EMG shows mixed neurogenic and myopathic features. Muscle biopsy shows intranuclear filamentous inclusions on EM, rimmed vacuoles, and TDP-43 positive inclusions. His father had dementia and bone pain. Serum alkaline phosphatase is elevated. What gene is most likely mutated?
choices: GNE (GNE myopathy — AR distal myopathy)
VCP (multisystem proteinopathy — myopathy + Paget bone disease + FTD)
MATR3 (matrin-3 — vocal cord and pharyngeal weakness + myopathy)
HNRNPA2B1 (multisystem proteinopathy type)
questions.explanation: VCP (valosin-containing protein) mutations cause multisystem proteinopathy (MSP), previously called IBMPFD (Inclusion Body Myopathy + Paget disease of Bone + Frontotemporal Dementia). Not all three features need be present, but muscle disease + elevated alkaline phosphatase + FTD in a familial AD pattern is classic. Biopsy shows rimmed vacuoles and TDP-43 inclusions — identical histology to sIBM but in a younger patient with family history and multisystem involvement. VCP encodes an AAA+ ATPase for protein quality control; mutations impair ubiquitin-proteasome clearance.

questions.question: A 25-year-old woman has bilateral symmetric sensorineural hearing loss, exercise intolerance, and a stroke-like episode at age 23 with a parieto-occipital lesion on MRI not conforming to any vascular territory. Lactate is 3.8 mmol/L. Her mother has diabetes and hearing loss. Genetic testing of muscle mtDNA shows 72% heteroplasmy for m.3243A>G. She is started on L-arginine. What is the rationale?
choices: L-arginine is an antioxidant that directly quenches reactive oxygen species in mitochondria
L-arginine is an NO precursor that promotes vasodilation, improving cerebral blood flow during acute stroke-like episodes in MELAS
L-arginine supplementation restores mtDNA copy number
L-arginine inhibits mitochondrial cytochrome c oxidase to reduce oxidative stress
questions.explanation: In MELAS, stroke-like episodes are thought to result in part from impaired nitric oxide (NO) production in cerebral vessels (due to arginine depletion), causing vasospasm and metabolic crisis. IV L-arginine (NO precursor) during acute stroke-like episodes has been shown to reduce their severity and duration. Oral L-arginine prophylaxis is used long-term. The m.3243A>G mutation causes mitochondrial dysfunction that disproportionately affects high-energy tissues (brain, cochlea, pancreas, cardiac muscle). Maternal inheritance and multisystem involvement are characteristic.

questions.question: A 19-year-old woman has had three episodes of painless optic neuropathy affecting one eye at a time over 5 years. Visual acuity is 20/200 in the left eye and 20/400 in the right. Brain and orbital MRI with gadolinium show no enhancement or demyelinating plaques. AQP4 and MOG antibodies are negative. What should be tested next?
choices: Repeat MRI with thinner slices to detect missed MS plaques
Mitochondrial DNA testing for LHON mutations (m.11778G>A, m.3460G>A, m.14484T>C)
Anti-AChR antibodies for ocular myasthenia gravis
Genetic testing for hereditary optic neuropathy (OPA1 — autosomal dominant optic atrophy)
questions.explanation: Sequential painless bilateral optic neuropathy in a young woman without MRI lesions and negative AQP4/MOG antibodies raises strong suspicion for LHON (Leber hereditary optic neuropathy). Testing for the three primary LHON mutations (m.11778G>A ~70%, m.3460G>A ~15%, m.14484T>C ~15%) on blood mtDNA is the next step. While LHON has male predominance (~80–90% of affected individuals), females do develop the disease. Idebenone (EPI-743 analog) is FDA-approved for LHON. OPA1 mutations cause dominant optic atrophy, which is bilateral and gradual rather than sequential and acute.

questions.question: A 16-year-old girl has 3 months of bilateral ptosis and fatigable diplopia. Ice pack test is positive. AChR antibodies are positive at 28 nmol/L. Thyroid function tests are normal. Chest CT shows an anterior mediastinal mass. What is the next appropriate step?
choices: Start oral pyridostigmine and observe the mediastinal mass for 6 months
Surgical resection of the thymoma (extended thymectomy) plus pyridostigmine — thymoma-MG requires thymectomy regardless of age
Initiate rituximab for thymoma-associated MG
Perform stereotactic radiosurgery of the thymoma first
questions.explanation: Thymoma-associated MG (approximately 15% of MG cases) requires surgical resection of the thymoma regardless of patient age, as thymoma is a neoplasm with malignant potential (WHO classification A, AB, B1, B2, B3). Extended transsternal thymectomy is the standard approach. Pyridostigmine is started for symptomatic management while surgical planning proceeds. Unlike non-thymomatous MG (where thymectomy age criteria apply), thymoma itself drives the recommendation — this is a cancer surgery indication, not just a MG management decision.

questions.question: A 33-year-old man develops severe myalgia and weakness in his thighs and shoulders after starting a new antiretroviral regimen for HIV. His CK is 18,000 IU/L. Muscle biopsy shows ragged-red fibers and mtDNA depletion. Which drug class is most likely responsible?
choices: Protease inhibitors (ritonavir)
Nucleoside/nucleotide reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs — particularly zidovudine/AZT)
Integrase inhibitors (dolutegravir)
CCR5 antagonists (maraviroc)
questions.explanation: Nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitors (NRTIs), particularly zidovudine (AZT), cause mitochondrial myopathy by inhibiting mitochondrial DNA polymerase gamma (POLG), leading to mtDNA depletion in muscle. The result is a mitochondrial myopathy with ragged-red fibers, COX-negative fibers, and mtDNA depletion on biopsy — indistinguishable histologically from inherited mitochondrial myopathy. Switching to alternative antiretrovirals (non-NRTI based) leads to gradual improvement. This is an important iatrogenic cause of mitochondrial myopathy.

questions.question: A 20-year-old man presents with exercise-induced muscle cramps, myoglobinuria, and a CK of 250 IU/L at baseline. Ischemic forearm exercise test shows no lactate rise AND no ammonia rise. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: McArdle disease (PYGM) — no lactate rise only
Myoadenylate deaminase deficiency (AMPD1) — no ammonia rise only
Combined glycogenolytic and purine nucleotide cycle defect — or technical failure of the test
Mitochondrial myopathy — both rises would be impaired
questions.explanation: In the ischemic (or non-ischemic) forearm exercise test: (1) No lactate rise + normal ammonia rise → glycogenolytic/glycolytic defect (McArdle, PFK deficiency, etc.); (2) Normal lactate rise + no ammonia rise → myoadenylate deaminase deficiency (AMPD1 mutations); (3) No rise in BOTH → either combined defect, inadequate effort, or technical failure (the most common explanation). When both are absent, the test should be repeated with careful attention to patient effort and ischemic occlusion. True combined defects exist but are rare.

questions.question: A 9-year-old girl has slow and stuttering speech, difficulty swallowing, and tongue atrophy. She has progressive facial weakness and nasal voice. She is otherwise cognitively intact. No limb weakness. EMG shows fasciculations and chronic denervation in tongue and facial muscles. What diagnosis should be specifically considered in a child with this bulbar LMN phenotype?
choices: Bulbar-onset ALS
Fazio-Londe disease / Brown-Vialetto-Van Laere syndrome (SLC52A2 or SLC52A3 riboflavin transporter deficiency)
Myasthenia gravis (bulbar form)
Kennedy disease (SBMA)
questions.explanation: Brown-Vialetto-Van Laere syndrome (BVVLS) / Fazio-Londe disease is caused by mutations in riboflavin transporter genes (SLC52A2 or SLC52A3), causing riboflavin deficiency in neural tissue. It presents in children/young adults with progressive bulbar palsy, sensorineural hearing loss, facial weakness, respiratory insufficiency, and LMN findings — but NO upper motor neuron signs and preserved cognition. Crucially, high-dose riboflavin supplementation can dramatically reverse or halt progression — making diagnosis urgent. This is a treatable motor neuron disease that mimics bulbar ALS.

questions.question: A 14-year-old boy with severe scoliosis has an FVC of 38% predicted in the upright position and 22% supine (orthopnea). He has Duchenne MD and is still ambulatory. What is the recommended respiratory intervention?
choices: Tracheostomy and invasive ventilation immediately
Nocturnal non-invasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV/BiPAP) — initiate based on respiratory criteria
Observation; initiate NIPPV only when FVC falls below 20%
Lung volume recruitment exercises only (air stacking)
questions.explanation: In DMD, current guidelines recommend initiating nocturnal non-invasive ventilation (BiPAP) when: FVC <50%, or when nocturnal hypoventilation is demonstrated (SpO2 <95% or end-tidal/transcutaneous CO2 elevation), or when orthopnea develops. This boy's FVC of 38% and marked orthopnea (>10% supine drop) meets clear criteria. Early initiation prolongs survival and quality of life. Tracheostomy (invasive ventilation) is reserved for failure of NIPPV or patient/family preference. Air stacking is a useful adjunct to maintain chest wall compliance but does not replace ventilatory support.

questions.question: A 50-year-old woman has a 15-year history of slowly progressive, painless weakness with wrist drop and foot drop. Her reflexes are diminished distally. NCS shows marked reduction in CMAP amplitudes with normal or near-normal conduction velocities bilaterally, consistent with axonal neuropathy. Her brother has a similar neuropathy. What gene is most commonly mutated in hereditary axonal neuropathy (CMT2)?
choices: PMP22 (CMT1A — demyelinating, conduction velocity slowing)
MFN2 (CMT2A — axonal, most common CMT2 subtype)
MPZ (CMT1B — demyelinating, variable age of onset)
GDAP1 (CMT4A — autosomal recessive demyelinating)
questions.explanation: CMT2A, caused by dominant MFN2 mutations (mitofusin 2), is the most common subtype of axonal CMT (CMT type 2), accounting for ~20% of CMT2 cases. The NCS pattern is axonal: reduced CMAP amplitudes with relatively preserved (normal or near-normal) conduction velocities, reflecting axon loss rather than myelin dysfunction. CMT2A can be severe with early onset. CMT1A (PMP22 duplication) is the most common CMT overall but is demyelinating. MFN2 encodes a mitochondrial fusion protein critical for axonal mitochondrial transport.

questions.question: You are evaluating a 5-year-old boy with a 3-year history of progressive proximal weakness. He has markedly elevated CK (15,000 IU/L), calf pseudohypertrophy, and Gowers' sign. Genetic testing of the DMD gene by MLPA and sequencing is negative — no deletion, duplication, or point mutation detected. What is the next appropriate step?
choices: Muscle biopsy is unnecessary; the diagnosis is excluded by negative genetic testing
Muscle biopsy for dystrophin immunostaining and Western blot — deep intronic mutations, structural rearrangements, or epigenetic changes may be missed by standard panels
Repeat MLPA in 12 months to check for somatic mosaicism
Diagnose as LGMD and begin rehabilitation only
questions.explanation: Standard DMD genetic testing (MLPA + Sanger/NGS sequencing) misses approximately 1–2% of pathogenic variants — including deep intronic mutations causing pseudoexon inclusion, complex rearrangements, and somatic mosaicism. In a phenotypically classic DMD presentation (age, CK, calf pseudohypertrophy, Gowers'), muscle biopsy with dystrophin immunohistochemistry and Western blot should be performed: absent or markedly reduced dystrophin confirms the diagnosis and permits targeted RNA-level testing (RT-PCR) to identify the causative variant. Whole-genome sequencing and RNA sequencing of muscle are increasingly used.

questions.question: A 30-year-old woman has a 4-year history of progressive fatigue, proximal weakness, and hair loss. She is found to have anti-signal recognition particle (SRP) antibodies and CK of 9,000 IU/L. Muscle biopsy shows necrotic and regenerating fibers with MHC-I upregulation and minimal lymphocytic inflammation. What is the diagnosis and recommended initial treatment?
choices: Polymyositis — prednisone 1 mg/kg/day monotherapy
Anti-SRP immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (IMNM) — combination immunotherapy with prednisone + IVIG ± rituximab
Dermatomyositis — prednisone + hydroxychloroquine
Inclusion body myositis — no effective immunotherapy
questions.explanation: Anti-SRP IMNM is one of two antibody-defined IMNM subtypes (anti-SRP and anti-HMGCR). It presents with aggressive, often severe weakness and very high CK, and responds poorly to prednisone alone. Current evidence favors combination therapy: high-dose prednisone + IVIG (monthly) as initial treatment, with rituximab added for refractory cases. Anti-SRP antibody titers correlate with disease activity. The muscle biopsy shows necrotizing myopathy without the dense inflammation of polymyositis — this distinction is critical, as treatment approaches differ.

questions.question: A 13-year-old girl has been evaluated for apparent 'stress-related' weakness. She fatigues rapidly during the school day but improves after napping. She has bilateral ptosis that worsens in the afternoon and a 'myasthenic snarl' (horizontal smile). Repetitive nerve stimulation shows 18% decrement at 3 Hz. She has no thymoma. Which test would be most specific for the diagnosis?
choices: Anti-AChR antibodies — if positive, diagnostic
Single-fiber EMG — most sensitive and specific test for NMJ transmission failure, even if antibodies are negative
Tensilon (edrophonium) test — most specific test available
Thyroid function tests — myasthenia is often thyroid-related in teenagers
questions.explanation: Single-fiber EMG (SFEMG) is the most sensitive test for neuromuscular junction dysfunction, detecting abnormal jitter and blocking in >95% of generalized MG even in seronegative cases. RNS decrement (already positive at 18%) supports NMJ disease, but SFEMG is more sensitive for confirming the diagnosis. Edrophonium (Tensilon) test has fallen out of favor due to cardiovascular risks. AChR antibodies are positive in ~85% of generalized MG but this patient may be in the seronegative 15%. SFEMG is the gold standard confirmatory test.

questions.question: A 65-year-old man has a 2-year history of wasting and weakness in both hands. He cannot extend his wrists or fingers on the right, and has prominent thenar wasting bilaterally. He recently noticed difficulty rising from a chair. There are NO sensory symptoms. EMG shows chronic denervation in C8-T1 distribution bilaterally and diffuse fasciculations including the tongue. He has brisk jaw jerk and bilateral Babinski signs. What is the most likely diagnosis?
choices: Multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN)
Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) — combined upper and lower motor neuron signs
Cervical myelopathy with bilateral C8 radiculopathy
Kennedy disease (SBMA)
questions.explanation: The combination of lower motor neuron signs (fasciculations, atrophy, denervation on EMG including bulbar region) AND upper motor neuron signs (Babinski, brisk jaw jerk) at multiple levels (bulbar, cervical, lumbar) fulfills El Escorial/revised Awaji criteria for definite ALS. Tongue fasciculations confirm bulbar LMN involvement. MMN is pure LMN without UMN signs. Kennedy disease is X-linked, has LMN only, and involves gynecomastia/endocrine features. Cervical myelopathy would not explain tongue fasciculations or brisk jaw jerk.

questions.question: A 45-year-old woman has a 2-year history of proximal weakness and Raynaud phenomenon. CK is 1,800 IU/L. Muscle biopsy shows perifascicular atrophy and perivascular inflammation with membrane attack complex (C5b-9) deposits on capillaries. Which myositis subtype does this pattern indicate?
choices: Polymyositis — endomysial CD8+ T-cell infiltration
Dermatomyositis — perifascicular atrophy and complement-mediated capillary injury
Immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy (IMNM) — necrotizing fibers with sparse inflammation
Inclusion body myositis — rimmed vacuoles and IBM inclusions
questions.explanation: Dermatomyositis (DM) is characterized histologically by perifascicular atrophy (type 2 fiber atrophy at the periphery of fascicles, closest to ischemic zones) and complement-mediated microangiopathy — MAC (C5b-9) deposits on capillaries reflecting humorally-mediated endothelial injury. This is the pathological hallmark distinguishing DM from polymyositis (which shows endomysial CD8+ cytotoxic T-cell infiltration) and IMNM. DM without skin rash (amyopathic or hypomyopathic DM) may present with muscle biopsy findings only.

questions.question: A 7-year-old boy has painless bilateral foot drop and distal leg wasting. His mother has mild bilateral pes cavus and areflexia. Nerve conduction studies show motor conduction velocity of 21 m/s uniformly across all tested nerves. Genetic testing shows a 1.5 Mb duplication at 17p12. What nerve biopsy finding correlates with this NCS pattern?
choices: Giant axonal swellings filled with neurofilaments
Onion bulb formations (concentric Schwann cell proliferation from repeated de/remyelination cycles)
Loss of large myelinated fibers with relative preservation of small fibers
Endoneurial inflammatory infiltrates and macrophage-mediated demyelination
questions.explanation: CMT1A (PMP22 duplication) causes uniform conduction velocity slowing across all nerve segments — the hallmark of a diffuse myelinopathy. Repeated cycles of segmental demyelination and remyelination produce onion bulb formations on nerve biopsy: concentric layers of Schwann cell processes around thinly myelinated axons. These are not specific to CMT1A but are characteristic of chronic demyelinating hereditary neuropathies. Giant axonal swellings are the hallmark of GAN (gigaxonin mutations). Inflammatory infiltrates are seen in CIDP.

questions.question: A 70-year-old man with a 3-month history of weight loss and proximal leg weakness has a CK of 280 IU/L, mild EMG myopathic changes, and a serum creatinine of 2.1 mg/dL. He has no rash. Anti-TIF1-gamma antibodies are positive. What is the most important next evaluation?
choices: Anti-Jo-1 antibodies to confirm antisynthetase syndrome
Age-appropriate cancer screening — anti-TIF1-gamma DM has a high association with malignancy
Chest HRCT for interstitial lung disease
Echocardiogram for myocarditis
questions.explanation: Anti-TIF1-gamma (transcription intermediary factor 1-gamma) antibodies are the myositis-specific autoantibody most strongly associated with malignancy — particularly in older adults. Up to 50–75% of anti-TIF1-gamma positive patients >40 years have an underlying cancer (most commonly ovarian, breast, lung, GI, and lymphoma). The combination of older age, constitutional symptoms, and anti-TIF1-gamma antibodies mandates comprehensive cancer screening (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, PET-CT, and gender/age-appropriate cancer workup). ILD risk is relatively lower in anti-TIF1-gamma DM compared to anti-MDA5.
